sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
अशोच्यानन्वशोचस्त्वं प्रज्ञावादांश्च भाषसे ।
गतासूनगतासूंश्च नानुशोचन्ति पण्डिताः ॥ ११ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
aśocyān anvaśocas tvaṁ prajñā-vādāṁś ca bhāṣase |
gatāsūn agatāsūṁś ca nānuśocanti paṇḍitāḥ || 11 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; vedabase renders the speaker tag inline without internal line-breaks)
exact (text body identical; renders single danda after speaker tag and after first hemistich, double danda only at verse end)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs slightly)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.011 = MBh_06,024.011). GRETIL writes 'prajñāvādān' as a compound without hyphen; vedabase renders 'prajñā-vādān' with hyphen; the akṣara sequence is identical.

anvaya.meaning: words of wisdom / talk of the wise
those whose life-breath has departed (the dead)
those whose life-breath has not departed (the living)
the wise (those who know the Self)

translations.verbatim_quote: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: While speaking learned words, you are mourning for what is not worthy of grief. Those who are wise lament neither for the living nor for the dead.
The Supreme Lord said: While you speak words of wisdom, you are mourning for that which is not worthy of grief. The wise lament neither for the living nor for the dead.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Lord at once took the position of the teacher and chastised the student, calling him, indirectly, a fool. The Lord said, "You are talking like a learned man, but you do not know that one who is learned — one who knows what is body and what is soul — does not lament for any stage of the body, neither in the living n[or in the dead]."

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (per Advaita Ashrama edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Bhisma, Drona and others are not to be grieved for, because they are of noble character and are eternal in their real nature. With regard to them, asocyan, who are not to be grieved for; tvam, you; anvasocah, grieve... Like one mad, you show in yourself this foolishness and learning which are contradictory.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads Krishna's first teaching utterance as a sharp diagnosis: Arjuna's grief and his learned-sounding speech are contradictory. The wise (paṇḍitāḥ) — those who know the Self — grieve neither for the living nor for the dead, because the Self is eternal. The reproach 'hence you are a fool' is taken at full force; Shankara does not soften the rebuke. The teaching anchors on the eternality of the Self: bodies come and go, but the substrate does not.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Lord said — You are grieving for those who do not deserve to be grieved for. You also speak words of wisdom about the nature of the body and the self... Hence, in you this contradiction is visible — your grief at the thought 'I shall slay them?' and at the same time your talk about righteousness and unrighteousness.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as opening the formal Self-teaching (ātma-vidyā). Arjuna's contradiction is named precisely: he grieves over the death of bodies even while speaking as if he knew the distinction between body and self. The wise — those who possess true knowledge of body and self — do not grieve for either category, dead or living, because the self is not destroyed when the body dies. Ramanuja, characteristically, immediately ties the eternality of the self to the duty of war fought without selfish desire — as a means to self-knowledge.

positions.rendering: words of wisdom, words used by men of wisdom, of intelligence — used here ironically: Arjuna's words sound learned but contradict themselves
speculative concoction (prajnavadah) — appealing to the mind but not according to Vedic injunction

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All major translators render prajñā-vādān as 'learned words' or 'words of wisdom', but the commentarial traditions diverge sharply on tone. Shankara reads the phrase as ironic — Arjuna's speech is shaped like wisdom-speech but is in fact contradictory, hence the 'fool' charge. Madhva goes further, classifying prajñā-vāda here as a kind of speculative reasoning that bypasses Vedic injunction. Modern translations flatten this into descriptive 'learned words' and lose the rebuke. The engineering layer must preserve the rebuke; this verse opens with Krishna calling Arjuna's reasoning misplaced, not merely 'learned'.

positions.rendering: the learned, the knowers of the Self — paṇḍā means wisdom about the Self; those indeed who have this are paṇḍitāḥ
those who know the exact truth of body and self as distinct

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The Sanskrit paṇḍitāḥ is technically narrow: not 'a learned person in general' but 'one who has paṇḍā — knowledge specifically of the Self.' Shankara grounds this etymologically; Ramanuja restricts it to those who have grasped the body/self distinction. Modern translators render generically as 'wise', losing the technical sense. The engineering analog should preserve the technicality: the operator who has internalized the architectural-invariant doctrine, not merely a 'wise person'.

literal_meaning: The Blessed Lord said: You have grieved for those not worthy of grief, while at the same time speaking words of wisdom. The wise grieve neither for those whose life has departed nor for those whose life has not departed.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna's first direct teaching utterance in the Gita. The verse opens with a sharp diagnosis: Arjuna's grief and his learned-sounding reasoning are mutually contradictory. The wise — those who have grasped the body/self distinction — do not grieve for either the living or the dead, because what is real (the imperishable substrate) is not destroyed by death, and what dies (the body) was never permanent in the first place. The verse is the chapter's pivot from corrective sharpness (2.2-2.3) to philosophical instruction. All major commentarial traditions agree on the diagnostic structure (contradiction between grief and wisdom-talk), and on the doctrinal claim (the wise grieve neither living nor dead because the Self is eternal). Tone of the rebuke varies: Shankara reads it as 'hence you are a fool'; Ramanuja reads it as the structured opening of the Self-teaching; Mukundananda and Prabhupada preserve the diagnostic without amplifying the rebuke.
